data.source: Large-scale online survey; stimulus items (jokes/memes/strips) extracted from Facebook pages and humorous strips
data.sample_size: 1751 participants (reported in Methods); summary also reports 1,903 participants in some sections (inconsistency noted)
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant ratings of items (participant-item level), with items and participants treated as crossed units
data.notes: Stimuli categorized into Covid Verbal, Covid Meme, Covid Strip, and NonCovid Verbal. Ratings collected on 7-point scales for funniness and aversiveness; perceived risk measured on 1-10 scale. Data have hierarchical/crossed structure (Participants, Items, Province). Some inconsistency in reported total N across manuscript summary.

method.description: Researchers collected humorous stimuli (Covid-related and non-Covid) from online sources, categorized them into four types, and administered an online survey during the Italian lockdown. Participants provided demographics, completed personality measures (Coping Humor Scale - CHS; Life Orientation Test - Revised - LOT-R), and reported psychological distance measures (perceived risk, spatial distance from outbreak epicenter, social distance). Participants rated each stimulus for funniness and aversiveness on a 7-point scale. Data were analyzed using linear mixed-effects models with crossed random effects for Participants, Items, and Province; models were fit hierarchically and compared via likelihood ratio tests.
steps: Collect humorous stimuli from online sources and classify into categories: Covid Verbal, Covid Meme, Covid Strip, NonCovid Verbal.

Design online survey including demographic questions, CHS and LOT-R scales, psychological distance measures (perceived risk 1-10, spatial and social distance), and item-level funniness and aversiveness 7-point ratings.
Recruit Italian participants online during the lockdown period (March 18–30, 2020) and obtain informed consent.
Present stimuli to participants and collect item-level ratings as well as personality and psychological distance measures.
Compute additional covariates (e.g., spatial distance from Codogno, province-level case counts) and scale scores for CHS and LOT-R.

Fit linear mixed-effects models predicting funniness and aversiveness with fixed effects for humor type, demographics, personality (CHS, LOT-R), psychological distance (perceived risk, spatial, social), and crossed random effects for Participants, Items, and Province.
Compare nested models (base model, +demographics, +personality, +psychological distance) using likelihood ratio tests and inspect contrasts between humor types.
Report estimated effects, test statistics (t, χ2), and interpret interactions/moderation by personality and psychological distance.
method.models: Linear mixed-effects models (hierarchical LMMs) with crossed random effects for Participants, Items, and Province; model comparison via likelihood ratio tests; contrasts between humor types
method.outcome_variable: Item-level Funniness ratings and Aversiveness ratings (7-point scales)
method.independent_variables: Humor type (Covid Verbal, Covid Meme, Covid Strip, NonCovid Verbal), Coping Humor Scale (CHS) score, Life Orientation Test - Revised (LOT-R) score, perceived risk of infection, spatial distance from outbreak epicenter, social distance (known cases among acquaintances), demographics (age, gender, education)
method.control_variables: Demographics (age, gender, education); random effects for Participants, Items, and Province (to account for clustering)

results.summary: Covid-19-related humor elicited higher aversiveness compared to non-pandemic humor. Memes were rated as funnier than Covid verbal jokes, while strips were rated as less funny. Personality variables moderated these effects: higher coping-humor (CHS) scores were associated with higher funniness ratings and lower aversiveness, while more optimistic disposition (LOT-R) was positively associated with funniness (notably for memes). Psychological distance influenced responses: higher perceived risk of infection increased aversiveness, and greater spatial distance from the outbreak epicenter was associated with higher funniness. Model comparisons via likelihood ratio tests showed significant improvements when adding demographics, personality, and psychological distance to base models for both funniness and aversiveness.

numerical_results.direction: positive (memes rated funnier relative to Covid verbal jokes)
higher aversiveness for Covid verbal relative to non-Covid verbal
lower aversiveness relative to Covid verbal
model fit improvement when adding demographics, personality, and psychological distance
model fit improvement when adding demographics, personality, and psychological distance

metadata.original_paper_title: Funny but aversive: A large-scale survey on the emotional response to Covid-19 humor in the Italian population during the lockdown
